claims.claim: Kubi Mensah (CEO, Gattaca/Titan Builder) published "What Institutions Should Know About Blockspace" on X on July 15, 2026, responding to Don Wilson's FT op-ed and IOSCO/ESMA/SEC commentary on MEV.
Titan Builder builds roughly 48-54% of Ethereum L1 blocks as of mid-July 2026, the largest single builder.

IOSCO's actual MEV-related documents (Dec 2023 DeFi policy report; Oct 2025 thematic review) treat MEV as a disclosure/control-risk factor for regulated intermediaries, not a finding that MEV constitutes market manipulation.

ESMA's strongest MEV language sits in a July 2025 research/TRV article ("may go against principles of fairness") and a May 2024 draft MiCA technical-standards consultation ("may be indicative of" market abuse) — both hedged, neither a final enforcement determination.

The SEC's April 13, 2026 staff statement cited in Mensah's piece is titled "Staff Statement Regarding Broker-Dealer Registration of Certain User Interfaces" and concerns DeFi front-end/wallet-interface registration status; it contains one hedged footnote about MEV-related disclosure but is not primarily an MEV/block-building determination.

An SEC Crypto Task Force staff memo (May 15, 2025) states searchers/block builders operating according to understood protocol rules are treated differently from alleged deceptive exploits, i.e. the SEC has not moved to treat ordinary MEV activity as manipulative.

United States v. Peraire-Bueno charges wire fraud and money laundering (not securities/commodities market manipulation) over an alleged $25M MEV-adjacent exploit; the case ended in a mistrial in November 2025 with no verdict as of the most recent reporting.

The nearest comparable DeFi-exploit criminal convictions (Eisenberg / Mango Markets) were fully vacated in May 2025 on improper-venue and failure-to-prove-misrepresentation grounds; DOJ's appeal was pending as of April 2026.

The UBS/Nethermind pilot announced June 23, 2026 consisted of two proofs of concept run on the Sepolia public testnet, with zero live transactions and no disclosed dollar volume — a feasibility demo, not a production deployment.
SUAVE, Flashbots' flagship unified-encrypted-mempool project, was discontinued and its repository archived on May 12, 2025 without ever reaching an Ethereum mainnet production launch.
Flashbots BuilderNet, a TEE-based collaborative block-building system, has been in live production since December 2024, with 100% of Flashbots' own order flow migrated to it.

A disclosed "TEE.Fail" attack (October 2025) demonstrated a hardware-level break of confidential-computing guarantees underlying BuilderNet's TEE model, theoretically enabling undetectable frontrunning; it requires physical hardware access and Flashbots disputes practical exploitability.

Shutter Network's threshold-encrypted mempool is in live production on Gnosis Chain but Ethereum-mainnet integration was still targeted for Dec 2025/Jan 2026 and the in-protocol EIP-8105 standard remained a first draft as of December 2025.

Budish, Cramton & Shim (QJE 2015) analyze a continuous-time, cross-venue latency-arbitrage problem among symmetric traders under a neutral, unforkable clearing utility — a different market-design category from Ethereum's discrete-slot, single-proposer PBS auction.

Academic literature on MEV-Boost/PBS argues it reintroduces a privileged-intermediary trust dependency (relays) of the kind batch-auction theory was designed to eliminate, and that even native on-chain batch auctions don't fully remove block-builder reordering power.

Lido's Relay Maintenance Committee approved Titan Relay in May 2024 only conditionally, explicitly flagging the vertical integration of Titan's builder and relay in one company as a conflict-of-interest risk requiring monitoring.

As of mid-July 2026, roughly 39% of Ethereum blocks were OFAC-compliant/censoring (up from a 2026 trough of 23.8% in April), against a historical post-Merge peak of 94.8% in October 2022; Titan Relay is currently classified non-censoring.

A peer-reviewed 2024 paper found the top 3 Ethereum block builders (beaverbuild, rsync, Titan) built roughly 80% of MEV-Boost blocks, driven substantially by exclusive order-flow deals that reduce genuine builder competition.
Beaverbuild, previously a leading independent builder, folded its order flow into the multi-party BuilderNet consortium during 2025, reducing the number of genuinely independent large builders.

Sandwich attacks on Ethereum caused an estimated $40-60M in annual trader losses in the Nov 2024-Oct 2025 window, with monthly extraction declining from roughly $10M to $2.5M over that period even as attack frequency stayed high.

Loss-versus-rebalancing (LVR) research estimates "benign" arbitrage MEV extracts upwards of $600M from Ethereum AMM liquidity providers, an order of magnitude larger than total sandwich-attack losses — a standing estimate rather than a fresh 2025-2026 measurement.

Industry analysis citing on-chain data estimates searchers typically pay more than 90% of gross arbitrage revenue upstream to block proposers/builders, functioning as a rent captured by a concentrated supply-chain layer.

Enshrined Proposer-Builder Separation (ePBS / EIP-7732), Ethereum's protocol-level fix intended to remove relay-trust dependency, is the headline feature of the Glamsterdam hard fork and remained in devnet testing as of mid-2026; the Ethereum Foundation's own Checkpoint blog describes implementation as "trickier than anticipated."

Kubi Mensah's own co-authored Multi-Party Block Construction proposal (ethresear.ch, May 27, 2026) states that "today's transaction pipeline has structural gaps," in tension with characterizing today's PBS system as a mature, institution-ready architecture.

Independent researchers (Decentralized Thoughts, Dec 2025) argue that even after ePBS ships, side-car bidding systems will still be needed because ePBS's staking requirement excludes smaller builders and doesn't fully resolve today's relay-latency and trust problems.

Gattaca (parent of Titan Builder/Titan Relay) was founded in 2018 in London, went through Y Combinator, and had roughly 19 employees and one disclosed funding round (2021 seed, led by G1 Ventures) as of mid-2026.
DRW Trading Group was founded in 1992 by Don Wilson and employs roughly 1,200-2,000+ people globally across all business lines; Cumberland is its dedicated crypto trading subsidiary.

The EU's PFOF prohibition under MiFIR Article 39a became absolute on June 30, 2026 when Germany's last transitional exemption expired, while the US SEC proposed rescinding Reg NMS Rules 611/610(e) (the order-protection framework) in June 2026 — opposite regulatory directions in the two jurisdictions most often cited as "TradFi's answer" to order-flow conflicts.

FINRA Rule 5310 imposes an enforceable best-execution duty on US broker-dealers that disclosure alone does not satisfy; no comparable legal duty currently binds Ethereum block builders, relays, or searchers.

The SEC's April 2026 Covered User Interface relief is structured so that qualifying providers must NOT exercise routing discretion or offer "best price" commentary — deliberately avoiding the kind of discretion that creates fiduciary/best-execution duty in TradFi.

The "blockspace as ordinal commodity" framing traces to Kubi Mensah's own April 2026 EthCC talk title rather than to prior transaction-fee-mechanism-design academic literature (e.g. Roughgarden), based on a search of that literature's own terminology.

As of this research pass, no independent public commentary or press coverage specifically addressing Mensah's July 15, 2026 article could be found — the debate is freshly published, not yet subject to visible third-party rebuttal.
